tip_title: Your Homeowners Policy Has a Cap on Jewelry, Cameras, and Gear. Most People Don't Know What It Is.
topic: Home Insurance - Scheduled Personal Property / High-Value Items
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Homeowners Policy Has a Cap on Jewelry, Cameras, and Gear. Most People Don't Know What It Is.**

Standard homeowners policies cover personal property, but there's a catch: for certain categories — jewelry, watches, cameras, musical instruments, collectibles, and sports equipment — your policy has a sub-limit, usually somewhere in the $1,000 to $2,500 range depending on the carrier. That means if a ring gets stolen or a camera gets damaged on a trip, your payout is capped at that sub-limit even if the item is worth five times more. The fix is a scheduled personal property endorsement, which lets you list high-value items individually and cover them for their actual appraised value. Summer is a good time to check this because people are traveling with gear, wearing jewelry to events, and hauling expensive recreational equipment around.

Think you might have valuables that aren't fully covered? Your Peachy agent can help you figure out if a scheduled endorsement makes sense for what you own.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/what-is-covered-by-standard-homeowners-insurance)
summary: Covers personal property sub-limits in standard homeowners policies for high-value categories like jewelry, cameras, and collectibles, and how a scheduled personal property endorsement closes the gap between sub-limits and actual item value.
scoring_notes: 78% homeownership rate in East Cobb, higher-income households (median $118k) are more likely to own jewelry, cameras, and recreational gear worth more than standard sub-limits. Prior home tips covered dwelling limits, wind/hail deductibles, water damage, wildfire, and home inventory — this is a genuinely fresh angle focused on personal property sub-limits and scheduled endorsements, which hasn't been touched. Summer travel and outdoor activity context makes it timely. Sourced from iii.org trusted domain.

tip_title: Road Trip Season Is Here. Does Your Auto Policy Travel With You?
topic: Auto Insurance - Summer Road Trip Coverage Gaps
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Road Trip Season Is Here. Does Your Auto Policy Travel With You?**

Summer means long drives, and most people assume their auto coverage just works wherever they go. The good news: your personal auto policy generally does follow you across state lines. The thing to actually check is whether your coverage limits are high enough if something goes wrong far from home — a serious accident in an unfamiliar city, a rental car after a breakdown, or towing across a long stretch of interstate can all expose gaps that never come up on a normal commute. If you've got kids who just got their licenses or college students borrowing the car this summer, now is a smart time to confirm they're properly listed on the policy too.

Not sure your coverage is set up right for summer travel? Your Peachy agent can walk through it with you before you hit the road.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/auto-insurance)
summary: Covers how personal auto insurance applies during summer road trips, including cross-state coverage behavior, limits adequacy for away-from-home incidents, and ensuring teen or college-age drivers are properly listed on the policy.
scoring_notes: Late June is peak road trip season. Both audiences have meaningful teen/college-age driver populations (East Cobb skews established families with teens). Auto coverage hasn't been touched in the last 6 months of covered topics. Highly actionable — readers can pull up their dec page and check right now. No candidates in the provided list covered auto travel gaps, so this fills a clear category hole while being the most seasonally urgent tip available. Source is iii.org, trusted domain. Note: no candidate in the provided list was a strong direct match, so this was sourced from iii.org's auto insurance resource as the best available trusted-domain fit for the audience gap.
